title: Daily Temperatures
description: Find number of days until a warmer temperature for each day

examples.explanation: Day 0 (30°): Check day 1 (40°) → 40>30 → answer[0]=1-0=1. Day 1 (40°): Check day 2 (50°) → 50>40 → answer[1]=2-1=1. Day 2 (50°): Check day 3 (60°) → 60>50 → answer[2]=3-2=1. Day 3 (60°): No subsequent days → answer[3]=0.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each day, iterate through subsequent days to find the first warmer temperature. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Solution for Daily Temperatures
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the O(n²) brute force approach to find days until warmer temperature for each day.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the Daily Temperatures problem by directly implementing the problem's core requirement: for each day, we need to find how many days we must wait until a warmer temperature occurs. The intuition is straightforward - we examine each day in sequence, then look ahead through all subsequent days to find the first occurrence of a higher temperature. This approach works because it directly implements the problem statement without any optimization. The algorithm proceeds as follows: 1) Initialize an answer array of zeros with the same length as the input temperatures. 2) For each day i from 0 to n-1: a) For each subsequent day j from i+1 to n-1: b) If temperature[j] > temperature[i], set answer[i] = j-i and break the inner loop. 3) Return the answer array. Time complexity is O(n²) because for each of n days, we potentially examine up to n subsequent days in the worst case (when temperatures are decreasing). Space complexity is O(1) excluding the output array, as we only use a constant amount of extra space. This connects to the full problem by being the complete, unoptimized solution that directly solves the original problem statement. Common mistakes include: 1) Forgetting to break after finding the first warmer day, which would incorrectly count multiple warmer days. 2) Off-by-one errors in loop boundaries. 3) Not handling edge cases like empty input or single-day input. The real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def dailyTemperatures(temperatures): n = len(temperatures); answer = [0]*n; for i in range(n): for j in range(i+1, n): if temperatures[j] > temperatures[i]: answer[i] = j-i; break; return answer

examples.trace: Day 0 (30°): Check day 1 (40°) → 40>30 → answer[0]=1-0=1. Day 1 (40°): Check day 2 (50°) → 50>40 → answer[1]=2-1=1. Day 2 (50°): Check day 3 (60°) → 60>50 → answer[2]=3-2=1. Day 3 (60°): No subsequent days → answer[3]=0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Finding First Warmer Day for a Given Day
ladders.desc: Implement the core inner loop that finds the first warmer temperature after a specific day.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine represents the most critical component of the brute force approach: given a specific day index i and the temperatures array, find the index of the first subsequent day with a higher temperature. The intuition is that we need to sequentially examine days after i until we encounter a temperature greater than temperature[i]. This works because we're implementing the fundamental search operation required by the problem. The algorithm: 1) Receive parameters: temperatures array and current day index i. 2) Initialize result = 0 (meaning no warmer day found). 3) For j from i+1 to end of array: a) If temperatures[j] > temperatures[i], set result = j-i and break. 4) Return result. Time complexity is O(n) in worst case when we examine all subsequent days. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 by being the inner loop that gets called for each day in the complete solution. Common mistakes: 1) Starting j from i instead of i+1 (would compare day with itself). 2) Not breaking after finding first warmer day. 3) Returning j instead of j-i (wrong units). Real code pattern: def findWarmerDay(temperatures, i): n = len(temperatures); for j in range(i+1, n): if temperatures[j] > temperatures[i]: return j-i; return 0

examples.trace: Day i=2 has temperature 75. Check j=3: 71≤75. j=4: 69≤75. j=5: 72≤75. j=6: 76>75 → Found at j=6. Return j-i = 6-2 = 4.
Day i=1 has temperature 40. Check j=2: 50>40 → Found at j=2. Return j-i = 2-1 = 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sequential Comparison Loop
ladders.desc: Implement a loop that compares each element with subsequent elements until a condition is met.
ladders.explanation: This core logic represents the fundamental pattern of searching through an array by comparing a reference element with each subsequent element until a condition is satisfied. The intuition is that many problems require examining elements in relation to their successors, and this pattern of 'compare and advance' is a fundamental algorithmic building block. This works by maintaining a current position and systematically checking each following element. The algorithm: 1) Take an array and a starting index. 2) For each index from start+1 to end: a) Compare current element with reference element. b) If comparison condition is met, return the result. 3) If loop completes without meeting condition, return default value. Time complexity is O(n) for scanning potentially all subsequent elements. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 by being the exact pattern used in findWarmerDay, where the condition is 'temperature > reference temperature'. Common mistakes: 1) Infinite loops from incorrect increment. 2) Off-by-one errors in loop boundaries. 3) Not handling the 'not found' case properly. Real code pattern: def sequentialCompare(arr, start_idx): ref = arr[start_idx]; for i in range(start_idx+1, len(arr)): if arr[i] > ref: return i-start_idx; return 0

examples.trace: Reference element = arr[1] = 3. Check i=2: arr[2]=7 > 3 → Condition met. Return i-start_idx = 2-1 = 1? Wait, correction: i=2, start_idx=1, so difference is 1. Actually the function should return the index difference. Let me trace properly: start_idx=1, ref=3. i=2: arr[2]=7>3 → return 2-1=1. But the example says output 2? Let me check: The problem expects days until warmer, which is index difference. For temperatures, if day 1 (index 1) is 3° and day 2 (index 2) is 7°, that's 1 day later (2-1=1). The example seems wrong. Let me use correct example: For arr=[5,3,7,2,8], start_idx=1, ref=3. i=2: 7>3 → return 1 (index difference). Actually the output should be 1, not 2. I'll correct: output should be 1.
Reference element = arr[0] = 10. Check i=1: 9≤10. i=2: 8≤10. i=3: 7≤10. i=4: 6≤10. Loop completes without finding element >10. Return default 0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Nested Loop Pattern for Array Processing
ladders.desc: Implement the pattern of processing each array element by examining all subsequent elements.
ladders.explanation: This building block introduces the nested loop pattern where for each element in an array, we examine all elements that come after it. The intuition is that many algorithms require comparing each element with every other element that follows it, creating an O(n²) pattern. This works by using an outer loop to iterate through each position and an inner loop to iterate through all subsequent positions. The algorithm: 1) Initialize result array of same length as input, filled with zeros. 2) For i from 0 to n-1 (outer loop): a) For j from i+1 to n-1 (inner loop): b) Perform some operation comparing element i and element j. c) If condition met, update result[i] and break inner loop. 3) Return result array. Time complexity is O(n²) due to nested loops. Space complexity is O(n) for the result array. This connects to L2 by showing how the sequential comparison gets applied to every element in the array. Common mistakes: 1) Using j from 0 instead of i+1 (wasteful re-comparisons). 2) Not breaking inner loop when condition met (if appropriate). 3) Index confusion between i and j. Real code pattern: def processArray(arr): n = len(arr); result = [0]*n; for i in range(n): for j in range(i+1, n): if arr[j] > arr[i]: result[i] = j-i; break; return result

examples.trace: Initialize result=[0,0,0,0]. i=0 (3): j=1: 1≤3; j=2: 4>3 → result[0]=2-0=2, break. i=1 (1): j=2: 4>1 → result[1]=2-1=1, break. i=2 (4): j=3: 2≤4 → no break, loop ends → result[2]=0. i=3 (2): no j (j starts at 4≥n) → result[3]=0. Final result=[2,1,0,0].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Array Elements with Conditional Break
ladders.desc: Implement element comparison in a loop with early exit when condition is met.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation focuses on the fundamental programming construct of comparing elements in an array and exiting a loop early when a specific condition is satisfied. The intuition is that many search operations require examining elements sequentially but stopping as soon as we find what we're looking for, which is more efficient than examining all elements. This works by iterating through array indices and using a conditional check with a break statement. The algorithm: 1) Initialize a variable to track result (default 0). 2) For index from start to end: a) Access array element at current index. b) Compare with reference value. c) If condition true, set result and break loop. 3) Return result. Time complexity is O(n) but often better with early exit. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 by being the inner loop body that gets executed within the nested loop structure. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting to initialize result variable. 2) Using wrong comparison operator. 3) Placing break in wrong location. Real code pattern: def findFirstGreater(arr, start, ref_value): result = 0; for i in range(start, len(arr)): if arr[i] > ref_value: result = i-start; break; return result

examples.trace: Initialize result=0. i=1: arr[1]=3 ≤3? Actually 3 is not >3, so condition false. i=2: arr[2]=7>3 → condition true. Set result = i-start = 2-1 = 1. Break loop. Return 1.
Initialize result=0. i=0: arr[0]=10≤25. i=1: arr[1]=20≤25. i=2: arr[2]=30>25 → condition true. Set result = i-start = 2-0 = 2. Break loop. Return 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Traversal and Element Access
ladders.desc: Basic operations of iterating through an array and accessing elements by index.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers the most fundamental operations needed for the brute force approach: how to traverse an array and access its elements. The intuition is that all array algorithms build upon these basic operations of moving through the array sequentially and reading values at specific positions. This works because arrays provide constant-time access to elements using indices. The algorithm: 1) Get the length of the array. 2) Use a loop to iterate from 0 to length-1. 3) In each iteration, access the array element at the current index. 4) Optionally perform simple operations with the element. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing the entire array. Space complexity is O(1) if not storing elements. This connects to L4 by providing the basic loop structure that gets enhanced with comparisons and conditional breaks. Common mistakes: 1) Off-by-one errors (using <= instead of < for array bounds). 2) Accessing indices outside array bounds. 3) Confusing array values with indices. Real code pattern: def traverseArray(arr): for i in range(len(arr)): current_element = arr[i]; # Do something with current_element

examples.trace: Array length = 4. i=0: access arr[0]=10. i=1: access arr[1]=20. i=2: access arr[2]=30. i=3: access arr[3]=40. End of loop.
Array length = 1. i=0: access arr[0]=5. End of loop.

approaches.name: Stack O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a monotonic decreasing stack to store indices of temperatures. When a warmer temperature is found, pop from the stack and compute the day difference. This efficiently processes each element once.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Daily Temperatures with Monotonic Stack
ladders.desc: Complete solution using a monotonic decreasing stack to find days until warmer temperature for each day.
ladders.explanation: The Daily Temperatures problem requires finding, for each day's temperature, how many days you must wait until a warmer temperature occurs. The brute force approach would be O(n²) by checking each subsequent day. The optimal O(n) solution uses a monotonic decreasing stack to store indices of temperatures not yet resolved. The intuition is that when we encounter a temperature higher than the temperature at the top of the stack, we've found a warmer day for that stacked index. We pop it, compute the day difference (current index - stacked index), and store it in the result array. This works because the stack maintains temperatures in decreasing order, so when we find a warmer temperature, it's the first warmer day for all popped indices. The algorithm processes each temperature once: either pushing it onto the stack or using it to resolve previous temperatures. Time complexity is O(n) since each index is pushed and popped at most once. Space complexity is O(n) for the stack and result array. This approach efficiently finds the next greater element for each index, a pattern applicable to similar problems. Common mistakes include incorrect index arithmetic, not handling the stack properly when empty, or misunderstanding the monotonic property. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the key subroutine of popping and computing differences when a warmer temperature is found.

examples.trace:
Initialize result = [0,0,0,0,0,0,0,0], stack = [].
Index 0 (73): stack empty, push 0 → stack = [0].
Index 1 (74): 74 > 73 (temp at stack top index 0). Pop 0, result[0] = 1-0 = 1 → result = [1,0,0,0,0,0,0,0]. Stack empty, push 1 → stack = [1].
Index 2 (75): 75 > 74 (temp at index 1). Pop 1, result[1] = 2-1 = 1 → result = [1,1,0,0,0,0,0,0]. Stack empty, push 2 → stack = [2].
Index 3 (71): 71 <= 75, push 3 → stack = [2,3].
Index 4 (69): 69 <= 71, push 4 → stack = [2,3,4].
Index 5 (72): 72 > 69 (temp at index 4). Pop 4, result[4] = 5-4 = 1 → result = [1,1,0,0,1,0,0,0]. Stack = [2,3]. 72 > 71 (temp at index 3). Pop 3, result[3] = 5-3 = 2 → result = [1,1,0,2,1,0,0,0]. Stack = [2]. 72 <= 75, push 5 → stack = [2,5].
Index 6 (76): 76 > 72 (temp at index 5). Pop 5, result[5] = 6-5 = 1 → result = [1,1,0,2,1,1,0,0]. Stack = [2]. 76 > 75 (temp at index 2). Pop 2, result[2] = 6-2 = 4 → result = [1,1,4,2,1,1,0,0]. Stack empty, push 6 → stack = [6].
Index 7 (73): 73 <= 76, push 7 → stack = [6,7].
End: result = [1,1,4,2,1,1,0,0].

Initialize result = [0,0,0,0], stack = [].
Index 0 (30): stack empty, push 0 → stack = [0].
Index 1 (40): 40 > 30. Pop 0, result[0] = 1-0 = 1 → result = [1,0,0,0]. Stack empty, push 1 → stack = [1].
Index 2 (50): 50 > 40. Pop 1, result[1] = 2-1 = 1 → result = [1,1,0,0]. Stack empty, push 2 → stack = [2].
Index 3 (60): 60 > 50. Pop 2, result[2] = 3-2 = 1 → result = [1,1,1,0]. Stack empty, push 3 → stack = [3].
End: result = [1,1,1,0].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Popping Stack on Warmer Temperature
ladders.desc: The core operation of popping indices from stack when a warmer temperature is found and computing day differences.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the monotonic stack solution. Given a current temperature and its index, and a stack storing indices of previous temperatures in decreasing order, we repeatedly check if the current temperature is greater than the temperature at the index on top of the stack. If it is, we pop that index from the stack, compute the day difference as current index - popped index, and store this value in the result array at the popped index position. This resolves that previous day's wait for a warmer temperature. We continue popping while the stack is not empty and the current temperature is greater than the temperature at the top index. The intuition is that the stack maintains a sequence of temperatures waiting for a warmer day, and the current temperature might be the first warmer day for multiple previous days if it's high enough. This operation ensures each previous day gets the smallest possible wait time. Time complexity for this subroutine in isolation is O(k) where k is the number of pops, but amortized over the full algorithm it's O(1) per element. Space complexity is O(1) extra beyond the stack and result. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each temperature in the main loop. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of resolving waiting days. Common mistakes include not using a while loop (only popping once), incorrect index arithmetic, or accessing temperatures with wrong indices. The pseudocode pattern: while stack not empty and temperatures[current_index] > temperatures[stack[-1]]: popped_index = stack.pop(); result[popped_index] = current_index - popped_index.

examples.input: Current index: 5, temperature: 72, stack: [2,3,4] (temps: index2=75, index3=71, index4=69), result: [1,1,0,0,0,0,0,0]
examples.trace:
Start: current_index=5, temp=72, stack=[2,3,4], result=[1,1,0,0,0,0,0,0].
Check stack top index=4, temp at index4=69. 72 > 69 → true. Pop 4, result[4] = 5-4 = 1 → result=[1,1,0,0,1,0,0,0]. Stack=[2,3].
Check stack top index=3, temp at index3=71. 72 > 71 → true. Pop 3, result[3] = 5-3 = 2 → result=[1,1,0,2,1,0,0,0]. Stack=[2].
Check stack top index=2, temp at index2=75. 72 > 75 → false. Stop. Final stack=[2], result=[1,1,0,2,1,0,0,0].

examples.input: Current index: 3, temperature: 60, stack: [0,1,2] (temps: index0=30, index1=40, index2=50), result: [0,0,0,0]
examples.trace:
Start: current_index=3, temp=60, stack=[0,1,2], result=[0,0,0,0].
Check stack top index=2, temp at index2=50. 60 > 50 → true. Pop 2, result[2] = 3-2 = 1 → result=[0,0,1,0]. Stack=[0,1].
Check stack top index=1, temp at index1=40. 60 > 40 → true. Pop 1, result[1] = 3-1 = 1 → result=[0,1,1,0]. Stack=[0].
Check stack top index=0, temp at index0=30. 60 > 30 → true. Pop 0, result[0] = 3-0 = 1 → result=[1,1,1,0]. Stack=[].
Stack empty, stop. Final stack=[], result=[1,1,1,0].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Monotonic Decreasing Stack
ladders.desc: The technique of keeping a stack where temperatures are in decreasing order from bottom to top.
ladders.explanation: A monotonic decreasing stack is a stack where elements (here indices of temperatures) are maintained such that the corresponding temperatures are in strictly decreasing order from bottom to top. This means the temperature at the bottom index is the highest, and each subsequent index has a lower or equal temperature. The core logic is: when processing a new temperature, before pushing its index onto the stack, we pop indices from the top while the new temperature is greater than the temperature at the top index. This ensures the decreasing property is preserved after the push. The intuition is that if the new temperature is higher than a temperature on the stack, that stacked temperature has found its warmer day (the new temperature), so we resolve it and remove it. What remains on the stack are temperatures that are still higher than or equal to the new temperature, maintaining the decreasing order. This property is crucial because it guarantees that when we later find a warmer temperature, it will be the first warmer day for all temperatures we pop. Time complexity for maintaining this property is O(1) amortized per element. Space complexity is O(n) in worst case. Connection to L1: This logic enables the popping subroutine. Connection to L3: This builds on the concept of stack operations. Common mistakes include not popping all eligible indices (breaking monotonic property), or popping when temperatures are equal (should not pop as it's not warmer). The pseudocode pattern: while stack not empty and temperatures[current_index] > temperatures[stack[-1]]: pop and resolve; then push current_index.

examples.input: Processing index 5 with temperature 72, current stack: [2,3,4] (temps: 75,71,69)
examples.trace:
Start: stack=[2,3,4] with temps [75,71,69], current temp=72 at index5.
Check top index=4, temp=69. 72 > 69 → pop 4. Stack=[2,3].
Check top index=3, temp=71. 72 > 71 → pop 3. Stack=[2].
Check top index=2, temp=75. 72 > 75 → false. Stop popping.
Push index5 onto stack. Stack=[2,5]. Now temps: index2=75, index5=72 → decreasing order (75 > 72).

examples.input: Processing index 3 with temperature 60, current stack: [0,1,2] (temps: 30,40,50)
examples.trace:
Start: stack=[0,1,2] with temps [30,40,50], current temp=60 at index3.
Check top index=2, temp=50. 60 > 50 → pop 2. Stack=[0,1].
Check top index=1, temp=40. 60 > 40 → pop 1. Stack=[0].
Check top index=0, temp=30. 60 > 30 → pop 0. Stack=[].
Push index3 onto stack. Stack=[3]. Now only temp 60 → trivially decreasing.

testCases.input: Processing index 1 with temperature 40, current stack: [0] (temp: 30)
Processing index 2 with temperature 50, current stack: [1] (temp: 40)
Processing index 3 with temperature 70, current stack: [0,1,2] (temps: 90,80,70)
Processing index 4 with temperature 65, current stack: [0,2,3] (temps: 85,80,85)
Processing index 0 with temperature 73, current stack: []
Processing index 5 with temperature 100, current stack: [3,4] (temps: 52,53)
Processing index 2 with temperature 50, current stack: [0] (temp: 50)
Processing index 3 with temperature 80, current stack: [0,1,2] (temps: 70,75,78)
Processing index 1 with temperature 60, current stack: [0] (temp: 70)
Processing index 4 with temperature 90, current stack: [0,1,2,3] (temps: 100,99,98,97)

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack Operations for Index Storage
ladders.desc: Using a stack data structure to store indices of array elements while processing.
ladders.explanation: A stack is a LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) data structure that supports push (add to top) and pop (remove from top) operations. In this problem, we use a stack to store indices of temperatures that haven't yet found a warmer day. The key insight is that by storing indices rather than temperatures, we can access both the index (for computing day differences) and the temperature (via array lookup). The stack helps manage which days are still 'waiting' for a warmer temperature. When we process a new day, we compare its temperature with temperatures at indices on the stack (starting from the top). This is efficient because we only need to check the top due to the monotonic property. The stack operations are: push(current_index) to add a new waiting day, and pop() to remove a day when its warmer day is found. We also need peek/top operation to check the top index without popping. Time complexity for each operation is O(1). Space complexity is O(n) for the stack. Connection to L2: This stack is used to maintain monotonic order. Connection to L4: This builds on basic stack operations. Common mistakes include using a stack of temperatures instead of indices (losing index information), or not handling empty stack cases. The pseudocode pattern: stack = []; push: stack.append(index); pop: index = stack.pop(); peek: stack[-1] if stack not empty.

examples.input: Initial stack: [], operations: push(0), push(1), pop(), push(2), peek()
examples.trace:
Start stack = [].
Push 0: stack.append(0) → stack = [0].
Push 1: stack.append(1) → stack = [0,1].
Pop: remove top → stack.pop() returns 1, stack = [0].
Push 2: stack.append(2) → stack = [0,2].
Peek: stack[-1] = 2. Final stack = [0,2], top index = 2.

examples.input: Initial stack: [3,4,5], operations: pop(), peek(), push(6), pop()
examples.trace:
Start stack = [3,4,5].
Pop: remove top → stack.pop() returns 5, stack = [3,4].
Peek: stack[-1] = 4.
Push 6: stack.append(6) → stack = [3,4,6].
Pop: remove top → stack.pop() returns 6, stack = [3,4]. Actually, let's correct: after push(6), stack is [3,4,6]. Pop returns 6, stack = [3,4]. So popped indices are 5 and 6. Final stack = [3,4].

testCases.input: Initial stack: [], operations: push(0), push(1), push(2)
Initial stack: [5], operations: peek(), push(6), peek()
Initial stack: [1,2,3], operations: pop(), push(4), pop()
Initial stack: [], operations: push(100), pop(), push(200)
Initial stack: [0,1], operations: peek(), pop(), peek()
Initial stack: [7,8,9], operations: pop(), pop(), push(10)
Initial stack: [3], operations: push(4), push(5), pop()
Initial stack: [6,7], operations: peek(), push(8), peek()
Initial stack: [1,2,3,4,5], operations: pop(), pop(), pop(), push(6)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Array Elements with Stack Top
ladders.desc: The fundamental operation of comparing a current array element with the element at the index stored on top of the stack.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two steps: first, accessing the temperature at the current index in the array; second, if the stack is not empty, accessing the temperature at the index stored on top of the stack, and comparing the two values. Specifically, we check if the current temperature is greater than the temperature at the top index. This comparison drives the entire algorithm: if true, we trigger the popping subroutine; if false or stack empty, we push the current index. The operation requires careful handling of indices: we use stack[-1] to get the top index, then use that index to access temperatures[stack[-1]]. Time complexity is O(1) for array access and comparison. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This comparison is used in stack operations. Connection to L5: This builds on array indexing and comparisons. Common mistakes include comparing indices instead of temperatures, or not checking if stack is empty before accessing top. The pseudocode pattern: if stack and temperatures[current_index] > temperatures[stack[-1]]: then pop and resolve; else: push current_index.

examples.input: Current index: 1, temperature: 74, stack: [0] (top index 0 has temperature 73)
examples.trace:
Given temperatures array: index0=73, index1=74.
Stack = [0], so top index = 0.
Temperature at top index: temperatures[0] = 73.
Current temperature: temperatures[1] = 74.
Compare: 74 > 73 → true.

examples.input: Current index: 4, temperature: 69, stack: [2,3] (top index 3 has temperature 71)
examples.trace:
Given temperatures array: index2=75, index3=71, index4=69.
Stack = [2,3], so top index = 3.
Temperature at top index: temperatures[3] = 71.
Current temperature: temperatures[4] = 69.
Compare: 69 > 71 → false.

testCases.input: Current index: 2, temperature: 50, stack: [1] (top index 1 has temperature 40)
Current index: 3, temperature: 60, stack: [2] (top index 2 has temperature 70)
Current index: 5, temperature: 100, stack: [4] (top index 4 has temperature 90)
Current index: 1, temperature: 50, stack: [0] (top index 0 has temperature 50)
Current index: 2, temperature: 80, stack: [0,1] (top index 1 has temperature 75)
Current index: 3, temperature: 65, stack: [0,2] (top index 2 has temperature 80)
Current index: 4, temperature: 95, stack: [3] (top index 3 has temperature 100)
Current index: 1, temperature: 20, stack: [0] (top index 0 has temperature 10)
Current index: 2, temperature: 30, stack: [0,1] (top index 1 has temperature 30)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Value Comparison
ladders.desc: The basic skill of accessing array elements by index and comparing numerical values.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves two parts: first, understanding that an array is a collection of elements accessible by indices (positions), typically starting at index 0. For example, in array temperatures = [73, 74, 75], temperatures[0] gives 73, temperatures[1] gives 74, etc. Second, comparing two numerical values using greater-than (>) operator to determine which is larger. In programming, we often access an element from one index and compare it with an element from another index. For instance, if we have index i and index j, we compare temperatures[i] and temperatures[j]. The result is a boolean: true if the first is greater, false otherwise. This is essential for the Daily Temperatures problem because we need to compare temperatures of different days. Time and space complexity are O(1) for these operations. Connection to L4: This is used in the comparison operation. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices, confusing indices with values, or incorrect comparison logic. Practice with simple arrays and comparisons builds intuition for the algorithm.

examples.trace:
Array indices: 0 → 10, 1 → 20, 2 → 30.
temperatures[0] = 10.
temperatures[1] = 20.
Compare: 10 > 20 → false.

Array indices: 0 → 50, 1 → 40, 2 → 60.
temperatures[2] = 60.
temperatures[0] = 50.
Compare: 60 > 50 → true.

approaches.name: Array Backward Traversal O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate from the end to the start, using an auxiliary array to store the next warmer day's index. This approach skips days by jumping to known warmer indices, optimizing the search.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Daily Temperatures with Array Backward Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete solution using backward traversal with index jumping optimization
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the Daily Temperatures problem by iterating from the last day to the first, using an auxiliary array to store the index of the next warmer day for each position. The key insight is that we can skip unnecessary comparisons by jumping to known warmer indices. Starting from the end, for each day i, we look at day j = i+1. If temperatures[j] > temperatures[i], we found the next warmer day. Otherwise, we check if result[j] is 0 (meaning no warmer day exists after j) - if so, we know no warmer day exists after i either. If result[j] is not 0, we jump to j + result[j] and continue checking. This jumping mechanism allows us to skip over days that we already know won't be warmer than our current day. The algorithm maintains O(n) time complexity because each day is visited at most twice - once when processing it, and potentially once when being jumped over. Space complexity is O(n) for the result array. This approach is elegant because it leverages previously computed information to avoid redundant work, similar to how dynamic programming builds solutions from subproblems. The backward direction is crucial because it ensures that when we process day i, we already have information about all days after i. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle the case where no warmer day exists (result should be 0), incorrect index bounds when jumping, and not properly initializing the result array. The connection to L1 is that this full solution implements the complete jumping logic described in the key subroutine.

ladders.title: Key Subroutine — Index Jumping for Next Warmer Day
ladders.desc: Finding the next warmer day by jumping through previously computed indices
ladders.explanation: This subroutine implements the core optimization of the backward traversal approach. Given a current day index i and the result array containing distances to warmer days for indices after i, we need to find the next warmer day efficiently. Instead of checking each subsequent day linearly (which would be O(n) per day), we use the result array to jump ahead. We start with j = i+1. While j is within bounds: if temperatures[j] > temperatures[i], we found our warmer day and return j-i. Otherwise, if result[j] == 0, there's no warmer day after j, so there can't be one after i either - return 0. If result[j] != 0, we jump to j = j + result[j] and continue. This works because result[j] tells us how many days to skip to reach a warmer temperature than day j. If day j is not warmer than day i, then any day that's not warmer than day j (which we skip over using result[j]) also won't be warmer than day i (since temperatures[i] >= temperatures[j] in this branch). The intuition is similar to path compression in union-find: we're building shortcuts to warmer temperatures. Time complexity for this subroutine is amortized O(1) per call because each jump moves us closer to a solution, and each index is visited at most twice. Space complexity is O(1) excluding input arrays. Common mistakes include infinite loops when result[j] is 0 but we don't break, off-by-one errors in distance calculation, and not handling the case where we jump beyond array bounds. This subroutine connects to L0 as it's the heart of the full solution, and to L2 as it implements the jumping technique that makes the algorithm efficient.

examples.trace: i=3 (71), result=[?,?,?,0,0,0,0,0]. Start j=4 (69): 69 <= 71, result[4]=0 → no warmer after 4, so no warmer after 3. Wait - incorrect! Actually at this point result[4] might not be 0 yet since we process backward. Let's trace properly: We're at i=3, processing backward so result[4..7] are already computed. Assume result=[?,?,?,?,1,1,0,0]. j=4 (69): 69 <= 71, result[4]=1 so jump to j=5 (72): 72 > 71, distance = 5-3=2. Return 2.
i=1 (80), result=[?,0,0,0]. j=2 (70): 70 <= 80, result[2]=0 → no warmer after 2, so no warmer after 1. Return 0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backward Processing with Precomputed Information
ladders.desc: Processing array from end to start using already-computed results
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental technique of solving problems by processing data in reverse order while leveraging previously computed results. The core insight is that when we process day i, all days j > i have already been processed, so we know result[j] for all j > i. This allows us to use that information to efficiently compute result[i]. The backward direction is counterintuitive but powerful: instead of asking 'for day i, find the next warmer day' (which looks forward), we effectively answer 'for day i, here's what we know about warmer days after it based on what we've already computed.' This transforms the problem from multiple independent searches into a single pass that builds knowledge incrementally. The algorithm structure is: initialize result array with zeros, then for i from n-1 down to 0: use the jumping subroutine (from L1) to find result[i]. The pseudocode is straightforward but the power lies in the direction. Time complexity is O(n) because the jumping subroutine is amortized O(1). Space complexity is O(n) for the result array. Common mistakes include processing forward instead of backward, not initializing the result array properly, and misunderstanding how the jumping works. This connects to L1 as it provides the context for the jumping subroutine, and to L3 as it relies on understanding array traversal directions.

examples.trace: n=8, result=[0,0,0,0,0,0,0,0]. i=7: last day, result[7]=0. i=6: compare with day7 (73<=76? No, 73<76), but actually we jump: j=7, result[7]=0 so no warmer after 7 → result[6]=0. i=5: j=6 (76>72) → result[5]=1. i=4: j=5 (72>69) → result[4]=1. i=3: j=4 (69<=71), result[4]=1 so jump to j=5 (72>71) → distance=2, result[3]=2. i=2: j=3 (71<=75), result[3]=2 so jump to j=5 (72<=75), result[5]=1 so jump to j=6 (76>75) → distance=4, result[2]=4. i=1: j=2 (75>74) → result[1]=1. i=0: j=1 (74>73) → result[0]=1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal in Reverse Order
ladders.desc: Iterating through an array from the last element to the first
ladders.explanation: This fundamental skill involves processing array elements in decreasing index order. While forward traversal (from i=0 to n-1) is more common, backward traversal (from i=n-1 down to 0) is essential for algorithms that need information about later elements when processing earlier ones. The key intuition is that when you're at position i during backward traversal, you've already seen (and potentially processed) all elements with indices greater than i. This allows you to use information about those elements to make decisions about element i. The algorithm is simple: initialize an index i to n-1 (the last valid index), then while i >= 0, process element array[i], then decrement i by 1. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing all n elements. Space complexity is O(1) for the iteration itself. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (starting at n instead of n-1, or using i>0 instead of i>=0), confusing when to use forward vs backward traversal, and not understanding why backward traversal is necessary for certain problems. This connects to L2 as backward traversal enables the core logic of using precomputed information, and to L4 as it builds on basic array indexing operations.

examples.trace: n=4. i=3: process array[3]=40. i=2: process array[2]=30. i=1: process array[1]=20. i=0: process array[0]=10. i=-1: stop.
n=1. i=0: process array[0]=5. i=-1: stop.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Accessing elements in an array using indices
ladders.explanation: This foundational operation involves retrieving or modifying elements in an array using their positional index. An array is a contiguous block of memory where each element can be accessed in O(1) time using its index. Indexing typically starts at 0 (zero-based indexing), meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. The last element in an array of length n is at index n-1. Accessing array[i] retrieves the element at position i. The key intuition is that arrays provide random access - any element can be accessed directly without needing to traverse from the beginning. This is crucial for efficient algorithms. The operation is simple: given array A and index i, the element is A[i]. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for the access itself. Common mistakes include accessing indices outside the valid range (i < 0 or i >= n), confusing zero-based with one-based indexing, and not understanding that arrays have fixed size. This connects to L3 as array indexing is necessary for traversal, and to L5 as it builds on the concept of arrays themselves.

examples.trace: Array has indices 0,1,2,3. Index 2 corresponds to the third element, which is 30.
Index 0 is the first element, which is 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: Basic introduction to arrays as indexed collections of elements
ladders.explanation: An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a fixed-size collection of elements of the same type in contiguous memory locations. Think of it like a row of lockers: each locker has a number (index) and contains one item. The key properties are: 1) Fixed size - you need to know how many elements it will hold when you create it. 2) Contiguous storage - all elements are stored next to each other in memory. 3) Random access - you can directly access any element if you know its index. 4) Zero-based indexing - the first element is at position 0, not 1. This is important because it affects how we calculate positions. For example, in an array of 5 elements, valid indices are 0, 1, 2, 3, 4. The index represents an offset from the beginning: index 0 means '0 positions from the start', index 1 means '1 position from the start', etc. Arrays are used everywhere in programming because they're simple and efficient for storing and accessing sequential data. Time complexity for accessing an element by index is O(1) - constant time. Space complexity is O(n) for storing n elements. Common beginner mistakes include thinking indices start at 1, trying to access beyond the array bounds, and not understanding that arrays have fixed size. This foundation connects to L4 as it provides the basic understanding needed for array operations.

examples.trace: The array has 4 elements. Position 1 in human terms is index 0 in programming: contains 10. Position 2 is index 1: contains 20. Position 3 is index 2: contains 30. Position 4 is index 3: contains 40.
Index 0 stores 'apple', index 1 stores 'banana', index 2 stores 'cherry'. There is no index 3 because the array only has 3 elements (indices 0-2).

testCases.input: Array=[true, false, true], what is at index 0?
